ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp food product class; no specific entry for salt-and-pepper fried small river shrimp. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns broadly; no specific code for salt-and-pepper fried freshwater shrimp preparation. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: seasoned with salt and Sichuan pepper
whole shrimp fried with shell on
tossed with aromatics (garlic, chilli, spring onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salt and pepper small river shrimp is approximately 200 g, presented as a shared plate for 2–4 diners.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15152); adapted for deep-fried preparation with salt and pepper seasoning using USDA FoodData Central fried shrimp reference (NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for 椒盐小河虾 (salt and pepper small river shrimp) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried shrimp (NDB 15150: shrimp, mixed species, breaded and fried) and plain cooked shrimp (NDB 15152), adjusted to reflect a lightly battered or un-battered deep-fried whole small shrimp preparation with salt, pepper, and aromatics. Carbohydrate is modestly elevated versus plain shrimp due to light coating and aromatics. Sodium is elevated due to salt seasoning. Fat is elevated due to deep-frying. Energy recalculated accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021; analogous estimate based on fried shrimp / seafood category (GI approximately 5)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 椒盐小河虾. GI is estimated at 5 based on the analogous category of fried shrimp and seafood dishes, which are predominantly protein and fat with minimal digestible carbohydrate. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) classify plain and fried shrimp/seafood as very low GI (approximately 5). Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (5 × 8.2 × 2) / 100 = 0.82, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salt seasoning and deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1040 mg per typical 200 g serving) represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salt and pepper small river shrimp has a very low glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 5) and low carbohydrate content (~8 g per 100 g), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, the high sodium content warrants caution in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from deep-frying, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation increases total fat and sodium content, which are unfavourable for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, noting that shrimp provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (~4.8 g per 100 g) that partially offset the cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 椒盐小河虾 (Salt and Pepper Small River Shrimp) is a classic Chinese dish featuring whole small freshwater river shrimp deep-fried until crispy and then tossed with a fragrant mixture of salt, ground Sichuan pepper, white pepper, dried chilli, garlic, and spring onion. The shrimp are typically fried shell-on, making the entire shrimp edible and providing a satisfying crunch. The dish is a staple of Chinese home cooking and restaurant menus across China and Singapore, where it is commonly served as a shared side dish or appetiser. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, aromatic, and mildly spicy, with the numbing quality of Sichuan pepper being a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of protein (~18.5 g per 100 g), provides moderate fat from deep-frying (~12.8 g per 100 g), and has a low carbohydrate content (~8.2 g per 100 g). The sodium content is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to salt seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ≈ 5), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. Shrimp is a natural source of selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free in its traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 椒盐小河虾 was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central under this exact dish name. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried shrimp data (NDB 15150) with adjustments for the specific preparation method. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for fried seafood. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Fish and seafood', which accurately reflects the primary ingredient (freshwater shrimp). 2. Cuisine tags in the input included 'Noodle', which is not applicable to this dish; this tag has been disregarded. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried shrimp reference used for adaptation. 4. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in USDA fried shrimp reference data. 5. GI is estimated at 5 (analogous estimate for fried seafood); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Sodium value (~520 mg per 100 g) is an estimate reflecting salt seasoning typical of this dish style; actual restaurant values may vary. 7. The price of SGD 28.80 from Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 (Boat Quay) reflects a restaurant-portion shared plate price and is not used in nutritional calculations. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried shrimp reference and are approximate.
